# Introduction

Multiple myeloma (MM) is a clonal plasma cell neoplasm characterized by the uncontrolled proliferation of malignant plasma cells within the bone marrow, leading to skeletal destruction, renal insufficiency, anemia, hypercalcemia, and immunodeficiency [@van2021Multiple]. As the second most common hematologic malignancy, MM accounts for approximately 10% of all blood cancers, with an estimated 35,000 new diagnoses annually in the United States and over 176,000 cases worldwide [@Padala2021Epidemiology; @Rajkumar2020Multiple]. The disease predominantly affects older adults, with a median age at diagnosis of approximately 69 years, and demonstrates a higher incidence among men and individuals of African descent. The global burden of MM has been increasing steadily, driven in part by aging populations and improved diagnostic ascertainment. Despite decades of incremental progress in extending survival, MM remains a largely incurable malignancy, and the clinical challenge of managing patients whose disease has become resistant to multiple lines of therapy represents one of the most pressing and consequential problems in contemporary hematology-oncology.

## Evolution of the Treatment Landscape

The therapeutic armamentarium for MM has expanded dramatically over the past two decades. The introduction of proteasome inhibitors (PIs), beginning with bortezomib and subsequently carfilzomib and ixazomib, fundamentally altered the treatment paradigm by targeting the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway essential to myeloma cell survival [@Manasanch2017Proteasome; @Dimopoulos2016Carfilzomib; @Moreau2016Oral]. In parallel, immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs) including thalidomide, lenalidomide, and pomalidomide became cornerstones of MM therapy through their pleiotropic mechanisms encompassing direct anti-myeloma activity, immune modulation, and anti-angiogenic effects [@Attal2017Lenalidomide]. The subsequent development of anti-CD38 monoclonal antibodies, notably daratumumab and isatuximab, represented another transformative advance. Daratumumab demonstrated remarkable efficacy both as monotherapy in heavily pretreated patients and in combination with backbone regimens across multiple clinical settings [@Palumbo2016Daratumumab; @Dimopoulos2016Daratumumab; @Lonial2016Daratumumab; @Mateos2018Daratumumab]. Isatuximab combined with pomalidomide and dexamethasone showed significant benefit in the relapsed/refractory setting in the ICARIA-MM trial [@Attal2019Isatuximab]. The anti-SLAMF7 antibody elotuzumab further expanded the monoclonal antibody repertoire, demonstrating activity in combination with pomalidomide and dexamethasone [@Dimopoulos2018Elotuzumab]. These three drug classes --- PIs, IMiDs, and anti-CD38 antibodies --- collectively transformed front-line therapy and substantially improved survival outcomes, with median overall survival now exceeding eight to ten years for newly diagnosed patients receiving optimal sequential therapy [@Rajkumar2022Multiple].

## The Challenge of Relapsed and Refractory Disease

Despite these therapeutic advances, virtually all patients with MM ultimately experience disease relapse, and the prognosis deteriorates with each successive line of therapy [@van2021Multiple]. The concept of "triple-class exposed" disease --- referring to patients who have received a PI, an IMiD, and an anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody --- has become a critical clinical designation, as these patients face progressively diminishing treatment options [@Gandhi2019Outcomes]. Patients whose disease is refractory to all three drug classes, termed "triple-class refractory," experience particularly poor outcomes, with median overall survival historically measured in months rather than years. The even more ominous designation of "penta-refractory" disease, indicating refractoriness to at least two PIs (e.g., bortezomib and carfilzomib), at least two IMiDs (e.g., lenalidomide and pomalidomide), and an anti-CD38 antibody (daratumumab or isatuximab), carries a median overall survival of approximately five to six months with conventional therapies [@Gandhi2019Outcomes]. Genomic studies have elucidated some of the molecular mechanisms underlying drug resistance, including mutations in cereblon (CRBN) and Ras pathway genes that confer resistance to IMiDs and other agents [@Kortm2016Targeted]. The urgent need for effective therapies in these heavily pretreated populations has driven an unprecedented wave of innovation in the field.

## Novel Immunotherapeutic Modalities

The treatment landscape for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma (RRMM) has been revolutionized between 2016 and 2026 by the emergence of several novel immunotherapeutic platforms. Chief among these are chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapies targeting B-cell maturation antigen (BCMA). The landmark KarMMa trial established idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel) as a viable option for patients with triple-class exposed RRMM, demonstrating an overall response rate of 73% with 33% complete responses [@Munshi2021Idecabtagene; @Raje2019AntiBCMA]. Subsequently, the KarMMa-3 trial demonstrated superiority of ide-cel over standard regimens in earlier relapse settings [@RodriguezOtero2023Idecel]. Ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel), evaluated in the CARTITUDE-1 study, achieved even more striking results with a 98% overall response rate and 80% stringent complete response rate, with durable responses observed at two-year follow-up [@Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene; @Martin2022Ciltacabtagene]. The CARTITUDE-4 trial further demonstrated the superiority of cilta-cel over standard-of-care in lenalidomide-refractory patients with one to three prior lines of therapy, expanding its potential role to earlier treatment lines [@SanMiguel2023Ciltacel]. Early-phase studies from multiple groups contributed foundational evidence for anti-BCMA CAR-T approaches [@Ali2016T; @Brudno2018T; @Cohen2019B; @Zhao2018A]. However, long-term follow-up data have revealed that remissions induced by BCMA-targeted CAR-T cells are often more limited in duration compared to CD19-targeted constructs in lymphoid malignancies, and mechanisms of resistance including biallelic BCMA loss have been documented [@Cappell2023Longterm; @Samur2021Biallelic].

Bispecific antibodies represent another paradigm-shifting class of immunotherapy, offering the advantage of off-the-shelf availability without the manufacturing delays inherent to autologous CAR-T products. Teclistamab, a BCMA-directed bispecific T-cell engager, received accelerated approval based on the MajesTEC-1 study, which demonstrated a 63% overall response rate in heavily pretreated patients [@Moreau2022Teclistamab]. Elranatamab, another BCMA-targeting bispecific antibody, showed a 61% overall response rate in the MagnetisMM-3 trial [@Lesokhin2023Elranatamab]. Talquetamab, targeting GPRC5D rather than BCMA, introduced a novel target to the therapeutic landscape and demonstrated a 73% response rate, providing an important option for patients with BCMA-directed therapy failure [@Chari2022Talquetamab].

Antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) represent a third novel modality, combining targeted delivery with cytotoxic payloads. Belantamab mafodotin, an anti-BCMA ADC conjugated to the microtubule inhibitor monomethyl auristatin F, demonstrated clinically meaningful single-agent activity in the DREAMM-2 trial for patients with triple-class refractory disease [@Lonial2020Belantamab], though unique ocular toxicity profiles have necessitated careful patient selection and monitoring [@Nguyen2023Mechanisms]. The nuclear export inhibitor selinexor, combined with dexamethasone, provided the first oral option for triple-class refractory patients in the STORM trial [@Chari2019Oral]. Looking forward, next-generation cereblon E3 ligase modulatory drugs (CELMoDs) including mezigdomide and iberdomide are in advanced clinical development, designed to overcome IMiD resistance through enhanced cereblon binding and more potent degradation of the transcription factors Ikaros and Aiolos, which are critical mediators of myeloma cell survival. These agents represent an important evolution of the IMiD class and may extend the therapeutic benefit of cereblon modulation to patients with acquired resistance to lenalidomide and pomalidomide.

## Rationale and Objectives

The period from 2016 to 2026 has witnessed a fundamental transformation in the management of RRMM, with an expanding array of immunotherapeutic agents offering new hope for patients with previously exhausted treatment options. However, this rapid proliferation of novel agents has created an increasingly complex therapeutic landscape that demands systematic evaluation. Critical questions remain regarding the optimal sequencing of these agents, the management of class-specific toxicities, the identification of predictive biomarkers for treatment selection, and the integration of novel therapies into earlier lines of treatment [@Dimopoulos2021Multiple; @Rajkumar2022Multiple].

This systematic review aims to address these knowledge gaps through the following objectives:

1. To synthesize the efficacy and safety data of anti-BCMA CAR-T cell therapies (ide-cel and cilta-cel) in RRMM, including long-term follow-up and real-world outcomes.
2. To evaluate the clinical evidence for bispecific antibodies (teclistamab, elranatamab, and talquetamab) as emerging treatment options for multiply relapsed MM.
3. To assess the role of antibody-drug conjugates, specifically belantamab mafodotin, and novel agents such as selinexor in the management of triple-class and penta-refractory disease.
4. To characterize the mechanisms of resistance to novel immunotherapies and their implications for treatment sequencing.
5. To examine the evolving paradigm of earlier integration of immunotherapeutic agents into the RRMM treatment algorithm.
6. To identify critical knowledge gaps and research priorities that should guide future clinical investigation in RRMM.

By systematically evaluating the evidence accumulated over the past decade across 50 studies published in 20 journals, this review seeks to provide a comprehensive and evidence-based framework for understanding the current state of RRMM treatment and to inform both clinical practice and future research directions in this rapidly evolving therapeutic landscape.
